steps.title: Stop the bleeding: build a $1,500 emergency wall ASAP
steps.description: You have $1,200 saved — that's 9 days of expenses. Add $300 this week to hit $1,500. Park it somewhere you won't touch it. This is your firewall so one bad day doesn't send you straight to the 22% card.
steps.impact: Breaks the emergency → credit card debt spiral. Without this buffer, every surprise resets your progress on the $4,500 CC balance.

steps.title: Avalanche the $4,500 CC — throw every spare dollar at it
steps.description: Pay the $135 minimum plus every extra dollar you can find. Even $265 extra/mo means you're putting $400/mo total at a 22% APR balance. That card is costing you roughly $82/mo in interest right now — kill it fast.
steps.impact: At $400/mo total, the CC is gone in about 12 months and saves you ~$500 in interest. At $500/mo, you're done in under 10 months.

steps.title: Audit your $3,800/mo in expenses — find $200 to redirect
steps.description: Your expense number is estimated, not confirmed. Pull 3 months of statements and find the leaks. Subscriptions, food delivery, random charges. Finding $200/mo in cuts accelerates the CC payoff significantly.
steps.impact: An extra $200/mo toward the CC cuts payoff time by 2-3 months and saves another $150+ in interest. The math is simple — find the leaks.

steps.title: Lock in a $0 interest deal on the $2,800 medical bill
steps.description: Medical debt at 0% interest is the least urgent — but call the billing office now and negotiate a lower lump sum or confirm your $50/mo plan in writing. Many providers will settle for 40-60 cents on the dollar.
steps.impact: Potentially wipe out $1,000+ of the $2,800 balance with a negotiated settlement, freeing up $50/mo minimum payment once resolved.

steps.title: Once CC is under $2,000, start stacking your emergency fund
steps.description: When the CC balance drops below $2,000, split your extra payments: half to the CC, half to savings. Target 1 full month of expenses (~$3,800) in your emergency fund before the CC is fully gone.
steps.impact: Reaching $3,800 in savings means you're no longer one car repair away from adding to your $57,300 debt pile.

steps.title: CC is dead — redirect $400+/mo to the car loan at 6.9%
steps.description: Once the CC is gone, you free up $135/mo minimum plus whatever extra you were throwing at it. Stack that onto the $320 car payment. Putting $500+/mo at the $15,000 car loan at 6.9% cuts years off the payoff.
steps.impact: Redirecting $455/mo total to the car loan saves ~$1,800 in interest and pays it off roughly 2 years early — then that $320/mo becomes yours.

rawResponseBody.overallMessage: You've got $57,300 in debt and $1,200 in savings. That's the whole situation. The good news: you have $400/mo in positive cash flow and you know exactly what you owe. The CC at 22% is the only fire right now — everything else can wait. Kill it, build a buffer, then work down the stack. This is fixable. It just requires 90 days of not blinking.
